cards.title: Objectives Resolution & The Three Constitutions
lines: Pakistan inherited the Government of India Act 1935 (adapted) as its interim constitution in 1947.
The Objectives Resolution (1949), moved by PM Liaquat Ali Khan, declared that sovereignty over the universe belongs to Allah.
It guaranteed democracy, freedom, equality, social justice, fundamental rights, and protection of minorities.
The 1956 Constitution declared Pakistan an Islamic Republic with a parliamentary, federal system.
The 1962 Constitution, under Ayub Khan, introduced a presidential system with the Basic Democracies electoral model.
The 1973 Constitution, framed by Z.A. Bhutto, was passed by consensus and restored parliamentary federalism; it remains in force today.
Key pattern (the pendulum): 1956 parliamentary then 1962 presidential then 1973 parliamentary.

cards.title: The Struggle for Pakistan (1905-1947)
lines: Two-Nation Theory: Hindus and Muslims were distinct nations in religion, culture, and way of life, deserving separate states.
Sir Syed Ahmad Khan revived Muslims through modern education in the Aligarh Movement.
Partition of Bengal (1905) by Lord Curzon benefited Muslims; its annulment in 1911 sparked Muslim political organisation.
The All-India Muslim League was founded in 1906 at Dhaka to safeguard Muslim political rights.
The Khilafat Movement (1919-24) aimed to protect the Ottoman Caliphate and built mass Muslim consciousness.
Jinnah's Fourteen Points (1929) demanded constitutional safeguards in response to the Nehru Report.
Allama Iqbal's Allahabad Address (1930) first proposed a separate Muslim state in the north-west.
The Lahore (Pakistan) Resolution (23 March 1940) demanded independent Muslim states; Pakistan was created on 14 August 1947.

cards.title: Dates You Cannot Get Wrong
lines: 1905: Partition of Bengal carried out by Lord Curzon.
1906: All-India Muslim League founded at Dhaka.
1929: Jinnah presented his Fourteen Points.
1930: Iqbal delivered the Allahabad Address.
1940: Lahore (Pakistan) Resolution passed on 23 March.
1947: Creation of Pakistan on 14 August.
1948, 1965, 1971: The three India-Pakistan wars.
1949: Objectives Resolution passed.
1956, 1962, 1973: The three constitutions of Pakistan.
1960: Indus Water Treaty signed with World Bank mediation.

cards.title: Key People and Their Contributions
lines: Sir Syed Ahmad Khan: led the Aligarh Movement for modern Muslim education.
Allama Iqbal: gave the Allahabad Address (1930) proposing a separate Muslim state.
Quaid-e-Azam M.A. Jinnah: presented the Fourteen Points (1929) and founded Pakistan as its first Governor-General.
Liaquat Ali Khan: first Prime Minister who moved the Objectives Resolution (1949).
Ayub Khan: introduced the 1962 Constitution and the Basic Democracies system.
Z.A. Bhutto: framed the 1973 Constitution by consensus.
Nawab Salimullah and Viqar-ul-Mulk: founders of the All-India Muslim League.

cards.title: Relations with India, USA, China & Neighbours
lines: Foreign policy protects national security, territorial integrity (especially Kashmir), and economic development.
Relations with India are dominated by the Kashmir dispute and three wars (1948, 1965, 1971).
The 1971 war led to the separation of East Pakistan and the creation of Bangladesh.
Relations with the USA were a strategic Cold-War alliance (SEATO, CENTO, War on Terror) but fluctuate with shifting interests.
China is an 'all-weather' friend; cooperation includes CPEC and major economic and defence projects.
Pakistan promotes peaceful borders with Iran and Afghanistan and works through SAARC and the United Nations.

cards.title: Agriculture, Water & Natural Resources
lines: Agriculture is the backbone of the economy: it provides employment, GDP, food security, raw materials, and export earnings.
Cotton is the major Punjab crop and supplies the textile industry, Pakistan's largest industry.
Irrigation depends mainly on the Indus River and its tributaries.
The Indus Water Treaty (1960), mediated by the World Bank, divided river waters between Pakistan and India.
Balochistan is the largest province by area; Punjab has the highest population density.
Largest forests are in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa; mangroves grow along the coast; Sui (Balochistan) is the largest gas field.
Energy is non-renewable (coal, oil, gas) and renewable (hydel, wind, solar); trade and transport connect producers to markets.

cards.title: Location, Provinces, Rivers & Mountains
lines: Pakistan has four provinces: Punjab, Sindh, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, and Balochistan.
The northern mountain ranges include the Himalayas, Karakoram, and Hindu Kush.
K2, the world's second-highest peak, lies in the Karakoram range.
The Indus is the main river, joined by the Jhelum, Chenab, Ravi, and Sutlej.
Lahore lies on the River Ravi; the Balochistan plateau uses the Karez irrigation system.
Climate ranges from arid deserts like the Thar to alpine mountains, shaping agriculture and settlement.

cards.title: Industry, Trade & Economic Challenges
lines: Major economic challenges include debt, unemployment, poverty, and inflation.
Wars increase defence spending and reduce foreign investment and aid.
Industry is classified as primary, secondary, and tertiary; cotton textiles is the largest sector.
Trade depends on exports such as textiles and rice and imports such as oil and machinery, affecting the balance of payments.
Sustainable development needs investment in renewable energy and infrastructure.
Education and vocational training are key to reducing poverty and unemployment.

cards.title: Youth, Environment & Governance
lines: Pakistan has a large youth population, offering a potential 'demographic dividend'.
Education and civic engagement turn youth into responsible citizens and drivers of innovation and entrepreneurship.
Climate change brings rising temperatures, floods, droughts, and water scarcity that threaten agriculture.
Deforestation and pollution damage ecosystems and public health.
Solutions include renewable energy (hydel, wind, solar) and strong environmental policies.
Poverty, unemployment, and weak governance require stable institutions and inclusive, sustainable growth.

cards.title: Population Dynamics & Employment
lines: Pakistan has a high birth rate, largely due to limited family planning.
Punjab is the most populous and most densely populated province.
Rural-to-urban migration is driven by jobs, education, and services in cities.
The largest employment sector is primary (agriculture).
A young, growing population can become a productive workforce if educated and employed.

cards.title: Nuclear Programme, UN & Global Organisations
lines: Pakistan developed its nuclear programme for security and strategic deterrence against India.
Nuclear status strengthens Pakistan's defence but increases international scrutiny.
Pakistan is an active member of the United Nations and contributes to global peacekeeping.
Regional cooperation runs through SAARC; Islamic solidarity is pursued through the OIC.
A balanced foreign policy lets Pakistan pursue peace, development, and regional influence.
